docs.title: LPB: funds given as loans or grants
docs.text:
Once you borrow money from public bodies, you can set a secondary policy, namely whether the money you get is given to your government as grant or as loan. Loans have to be paid back, over time, and you have to pay interest to the lender.

Say you borrow 100 Euros, for 3 years, with an interest of 3% per year. If you repay once a year, you have to pay 35.25€ each year, for a total of 106.06€. So the interest is 6.06€. You can do your own calculations here.

This policy lets you decide if all the money given to you is a loan (set the slider to 0) or a grant (set the slider to 100), or anywhere between. 50 means half as loan and half as grant, 30 means 30% as loan and 70% as grant.

Note: Setting this slider only makes sense if you set the Lending from public bodies policy to something other than 0.

docs.title: LPB: Split the use of funds from public lenders
docs.text:
A further decision you need to take after you get money from public lenders either as loan or grant, is what you will use the money for.

There are two possible uses: You can invest the money, or you can consume it. Investing means spending the money on education, on security, on rail and roads, on health care, in other words, on things that last and need to be handled on the level of society. Consuming means spending money on transfers to poor people, on subsidizing (= supporting financially) rents, fuel, farmers, low income earners. This is often money that goes to individual people.

The difference between investing and consuming is that investing usually has a long term benefit - when you learn today, you can apply what you learned for a long time. When you invest in your health by jogging a bit every day, you will benefit again for a long time. Consuming is done here and now and there is, usually, no long term benefit. A bit like the saying about giving people fish to eat (consumption) and teaching them how to fish (investing).

In this policy you can set the slider to 0 (= all conumption) or 100 (= all investing) or anywhere in between.

Note: Setting this slider only makes sense if you set the Lending from public bodies policy to something other than 0.

docs.title: Max forest cutting
docs.text:
Forests, especially rain forests, are cut at high rates around the world. Details are here. This is bad for several reasons:

  1. Forests store roughly half their weight as CO2, when they are cut, this is released to the atmosphere, making global warming worse.
  2. The ground they stand on also contains CO2, when they are cut, this is washed out, ending up in the oceans and the air, both of which is bad. (Bonus point: Do you know why?)
  3. They are great storage and retreats for biodiversity, when they are cut, this disappears.
  4. Can you think of anything else?

You can reduce or even stop forest cutting with this policy. 0 means no limitation on cutting - things go on as before; 10=10% reduction in the maximum amount that can be cut, 50=50% reduction in max cut, etc. all the way to 90 % reduction which is practically a ban on cutting.

docs.title: Off-balance sheet financing
docs.text:
You can find good explanations for the use of off-balance sheet financing in companies here and here. For more details, follow the links on these pages.

Increasingly, governments are also known to use this form of financing. See here and here. For more details, follow the links on these pages.

Since this is a complicated subject, you might, as the game master or mistress, ask one or more of your players to prepare a short presentation of the concept and its advantages and disadvantages for the other players.

As a player, you could vonlunteer to prepare a short presentation of the concept and its advantages and disadvantages for your fellow players.

Note: if you read this in a language other than English and you know of links similar to the ones above in your language, drop us a line at simfuture@blue-way.net. Or if you have a better explanation, again, drops us a line.

docs.title: Pensions to all
docs.text:
To fight poverty in old age you can introduce pensions for all.

The size of the pension is expressed as the percent of the GDP you want to invest. 0 means you invest nothing and leave things as they are, including leaving old people poor. 5 means you invest 5% of GDP; 10 = 10% of GDP money is transferred to workers (=the poor) and paid for by owners (=the rich).

docs.title: People
docs.text: You need 1 game master or mistress and up to 60 players. The game is divided into 10 geographical regions, each with 6 roles.

docs.title: Decisions are made by people or the app
docs.text:
In each region, either all ministries must be assigned to human players, or none. In other words, there cannot be a region where only 4 ministries are assigned to human players - or 3, or 5, and so on. So you need 6, 12, 18, 24, 30, 36, 42, 48, 54 or 60 players, because each region must be played either entirely by humans or entirely by the app. So if you have a number of people that is not divisible by 6, you should assign 2 people to some roles.

The roles played by the app automatically make random political decisions, where the bottom 10% and the top 10% are cut off: if a policy has a range from 0 to 100, then the decision is randomly chosen to be between 10 and 90.

If the game rounds are played on different days, the same people do not have to be present, but you must have the same number of people so that all roles originally assigned to human players will continue to be played by human players.

The regions are:

  1. China
  2. Africa, south of the Sahara
  3. Middle East and North Africa
  4. Latin America
  5. Pacific Rim (countries bordering the Pacific: Canada, Japan, New Zealand, Australia, South Korea)
  6. South Asia (India, Pakistan, Bangladesh)
  7. Southeast Asia
  8. Eastern Europe and Central Asia
  9. The USA
  10. Europe

Each region has the following roles:

  1. Minister for the Future
  2. Minister against Poverty
  3. Minister against Inequality
  4. Minister for Equality (Empowerment)
  5. Minister for Food and Agriculture
  6. Minister for Energy

and a game organiser, in a school setting, this is the teacher.

docs.title: Place
docs.text:
You need a room that is big enough for all the players. The players should be grouped together in their regions. How much space you leave between the regions depends on the size of the room. Whether the groups have tables or not is up to you - it depends a bit on the devices the players are using: Laptops and PCs benefit from tables, see Technology.

If the weather is good and there is a quiet spot outside, you can also go outside - as long as the internet connection remains stable. The screen and projector for the game organiser could become a problem outside, see Technology.

docs.title: Policies, in depth
docs.text: In this section, all the policies players can change in the game, are explained in a bit more depth.

docs.title: Policy Sliders
docs.text:
Each ministry, except the Ministry for the Future, decides via several policies how much to invest in the future to improve the lives of the people in their region. For each policy you choose how much you invest by setting a slider somewhere between its minimum and maximum value. Each policy also has a drop-down that you can open for a short explanation of what the policy is and what it does. Finally, each policy has an entry in this help manual with more information.

Investments cost money. How much, the Minister for the Future can tell. He or she sees in real time the cost implications of each decision (slider move) the Minister for each portfolio takes. The Minister for the Future also sees how much the sum of all investment plans of his or her portfolio minister in their region are. The game app automatically compares this total with the maximum budget (= 2% of the GDP of the region) the region has to invest. If the total regional investment plans are below the budget, then the Future Minister can submit the porposals to the game organizer. Note: once he or she does this, the portfolio ministers may still adjust their slider settings, but those changes are no longer taken into account for the current round!

What do the minimum and maximum values mean? That depends on the variable. Let's take as an example Electrify everything, a policy that the Minister for Energy has to decide. See also the glossary entry. Everything in this context means all the energy use currently still fueled by fossil fuels: cars, trucks, trains, planes and ships still using petrol or diesel; heating with oil or gas; industrial processes like making cement. As Minister for Energy, you can decide that you want to replace all this fossil fuel with electriciy. By how much? The minimum is set at 0, if you choose this, you actually don't change anything, you leave things as they are. The maximum is set at 95%, because there will be some uses of oil, gas or coal that cannot be replaced by electricity for technical or physical resons.

docs.title: The Cost of Investment
docs.text: Investments cost money: When you set the slider at its minimum, you spend nothing. If you set the slider at its maximum, you spend the entire cost of that policy. If you set it anywhere in between, you spend the prorated cost. Prorated? If you set the slider at exactely half between min and max, you spend half the cost, set it at 2/3 between min and max, you spend 2/3 of the cost, and so on.

docs.title: Maximum cost
docs.text: The maximum cost of each policy is calculated as follows: One of the original developers of the simulation model behind the game, Per Espen Stoknes, estimated that each turnaround (these are the Ministries in the game) for Giant Leap would cost, world-wide, 1 Tera Dollar per year for Energy, Food & Agriculture, Empowerment and Poverty each; and 4 Tera Dollar per year for Inequality. Tera = 10^12 = 1 000 000 000 000. The GDP of the world is about 120 Tera Dollars in 2025. So the turnarounds cost a maximum of 8/120 = 7 % of the world's GDP. Now, cost is a misleading label, because this money does not disappear into thin air. It is instead invested, i.e. spent on innovation, re-training, building new things, wages for workers, etc. Still, if it is spent on Giant Leap, it is for starters not available for something else, hence people think of it as a cost.

docs.title: Distribution of global costs across regions
docs.text: In the game we take this global cost and distribute it over the 10 regions, based on the regions' relative weight of GDP. This means that a poor region pays less for a poverty (and any other) turnaround than a rich region. The reason is that in a rich region wages are higher, rents are higher, food cost is higher, etc. The next step in calculating the cost of a policy is to divide the cost for each entire turnaround by the number of policies in that turnaround (= ministry). This is, admittedly, a rough estimate, if you have a better idea, please send an email to simfuture@blue-way.net.

docs.title: Two modifications
docs.text:
At last, we then modify this cost by two influences:

  • 1) the environmental damage in the region - the higher the damage, the higher the investment cost. The logic here is that, for example, extreme weather doesn't occur just once every hundred years, but more like once every 10 years! Thus, you have to clean up and repair more often, build stronger houses, better roads and rail...
  • 2) the reform willingness in the region - the more willing, the less the investment cost. If people are more willing to try new things, the less you have to spend because thing get done faster, with fewer objections, and with more people working together, rather than against each other.

docs.title: Privacy
docs.text:
No personal data is stored and all game data is deleted 90 days after the last login to a game.

The "state" of the game is saved continuously for each individual. So if someone accidentally leaves the game app, he or she can log in again with the correct game-ID and their username and automatically return to the page he or she was on when he or she left. The username can be chosen freely, including fantasy names. But do make a note of it! Continuous saving also makes it possible to play the three rounds at different times; even on different days or weeks, see Rounds.

The game organiser has a button to display all player IDs in case a player has forgotten what he or she called him or herself when choosing their username.

docs.title: Reduce food imports
docs.text:
Much of the food we eat is imported, where even the net exporters do import food - they just export more than they import.

You can change this by reducing imports in your region. That does not mean your people will go hungry, it just means that food will be produced locally and that less grain, pigs, chicken, tomatoes, cows, etc. will be shipped half way around the world.

If you set the policy to 0, you reduce nothing, i.e. food will be exported and imported as now. 10=10% reduction, 50=50% reduction (the maximum).

docs.title: Reforestation
docs.text:
Shrinking forests is bad, but you can plant trees as a policy to work against this. This is called reforestation.

0 means no reforestation, 1 means you increase the forest area by 1% per year, 3 = you increase the forest area by 3% per year ( in 10 years you have increase the forest land by about 34% (!) - as long as there is land that can be reforested!)

docs.title: Regenerative agriculture
docs.text:
We are loosing ground - the land we need to grow our food. And we spend ridiculous amounts of energy to get our food.

But you can do something about it: with this policy you decide the percentage of your cropland that is worked regeneratively (=low or no tillage, low or no fertilizers and pesticides etc.) 50 means 50% cropland worked is regeneratively, 100 = 100% of cropland is worked regeneratively, etc. 0 leaves things as industrial they are.

docs.title: Three rounds of play
docs.text: The game consists of 3 decision rounds. At the start of the game, information is displayed in graphic form (see How do I read a graph?) from 1990 to 2025 for each region and each ministry.

docs.title: New information
docs.text: When there is new information, the players should look at and discuss the results, first in their region and then across regions. For example, all energy ministers could meet and discuss energy issues, all poverty ministers could meet and discuss poverty issues, etc.

docs.title: Review finished
docs.text: When the review of the past is complete, players must make decisions relevant to their respective ministry. Each minister is shown several investment possibilities that he or she can make. Each investment measure is briefly explained and they can decide, in consultation with their fellow ministers, to invest a certain amount to hopefully improve the lives of the citizens of their region. He or she determines the amount to be invested by moving a slider.

docs.title: Discussions and negotiations, face to face
docs.text:
Each measure carries an investment costs and the Minister of the Future looks at the total amount that his or her regional colleagues intend to invest. If this amount is below the budget (which is shown to the Minister of the Future), everything is fine and he or she can submit the decisions for the region for the current round. However, if the total amount of the investment plans exceeds the budget, the ministers in the region must renegotiate their plans until the total amount is within the budget. Only then can the Minister for the Future submit the decisions. These discussions and negotiations are at the heart of the game, forcing players to argue face-to-face in favour of their decisions, weigh up the pros and cons of a particular investment plan and compromise in order to move forward.

Once all regions (played by human players) have submitted their decisions, the game organizer lets the game continue.

docs.title: The time horizons
docs.text:
The first round, in which the decisions are made in the current year, runs until the year 2040 - so it is the future of the players themselves.

The second round, in which the decisions are made in 2040, runs until 2060 - it is therefore the future of the players' children.

The third and final round, in which the decisions are made in the year 2060, runs until the year 2100 - it is therefore the future of the players' grandchildren.

docs.title: Shift govt spending to investment
docs.text:
Governments choose how to use their spending: primarily for consumption (eg child support, subsidies for food or energy, etc.) or for public investment (education, health care, infrastructure etc.)

This policy shifts spending from consumption to investment. 0 means no shift, 10=10% of consumption shifted to investment, 25=25 % of consumption shifted to investment, etc. You can shift at most 50 % of consumption to investment.

See also LPB: Split the use of funds from public lenders.

docs.title: Strengthen Unions
docs.text:
In any economy, the national income is shared between owners and workers. The sharing is done through negotiation between the two. Take a look at the share going to workers (= labor) in the US. Since 1948, the share has been going down and down and down. The source of this image is here. Currently it is here: The situation is similar in other countries. What does the graph tell you?

You can give the workers (= the ones who barely live from paycheck to paycheck) a lift: First, you can strengthen unions and union legislation. This gives workers a bigger share of the national income. The policy you set is a percentage increase in the share. 0 leaves things as they are, you change nothing. 1 increases the share by 1%, 2 by 2% and 3 by 3%. This may not sound like much but if you increase the workers' share from 50% to 51% (which an increase of 2 % is), you have at the same time reduced the owners' share from 50% to 49%. You've begun to tip the balance.

See also Worker reaction

docs.title: Stretch repayment
docs.text:
When you borrow money, you agree to pay back the loan with a certain interest rate over an agreed time. For a consumer loan (for example for a vacation) this is usually a year or two. For a house, this might be as long as 30 years. The longer the repayment duration, the lower your periodic payment (with the same amount borrowed). Thus, when you are short of money, you may want to stretch your repayment. From 3 to 5 years, or from 20 to 30 years.

This is the policy that lets you do that. Setting it to 1, the default, leaves things as they are. Setting it to 2 doubles your repayment duration, etc.

docs.title: Taxing Owners Wealth
docs.text:
There are some seriously rich people in this world. And they got there because they pay less tax than you do.

So, one policy in this game is to tax them. You decide how much, anywhere from 0% (= you leave them as rich as they currently are) to 80% (= you take each year 80% of the wealth they have accumuated away, so your government can invest in education, security, roads, ...). Or, as always, anywhere in between: 5%, 12%, 25%, etc...

If you want to dig deeper: look here, or here, or here, or just google taxing the rich.

docs.title: Technology
docs.text:
You need a good internet connection, because everyone will be connected to the internet at the same time.

Any modern browser with any device (mobile phone, tablet, laptop, PC) will work, but things look a bit small on mobile phones.

A projector and screen can be provided for the game organizer, as he or she will be shown the global results (the players will only be shown the results for their region and ministry). The game organizer may want to discuss how individual regional decisions have a global impact.

docs.title: The Energy Cost of Feeding the World
docs.text: How much energy does it take to produce the food we eat? The answer has changed dramatically over the last 200 years — and not in a good direction.

docs.title: Traditional Farming
docs.text: For most of human history, farming was powered by the sun. Seeds, rain, human muscle and animal power were the inputs. The energy return was positive: for every calorie of effort put in, farmers harvested roughly 5 to 10 calories of food. This ratio is called the Energy Return on Investment (EROI).

docs.title: The Industrial Turn
docs.text:
From around 1900 onwards, and accelerating after World War II, farming became industrialised. Tractors replaced horses, synthetic fertilisers replaced manure, pesticides replaced manual weeding, and irrigation pumps replaced rain. All of these inputs run on fossil fuels — oil, gas and coal.

The result: the EROI of agriculture flipped. Today, modern industrial farming typically requires 5 to 15 calories of fossil fuel energy to produce a single calorie of food on your plate — when you count the full chain from field to fork: fuel for machinery, energy to produce fertiliser, pesticides, irrigation, transport, processing and refrigeration.

docs.title: Why Fertiliser Matters So Much
docs.text: The single biggest energy cost is nitrogen fertiliser. Producing it requires the Haber-Bosch process, which uses natural gas as both a raw material and an energy source. Roughly 1–2 litres of diesel equivalent are needed to produce just 1 kg of nitrogen fertiliser. Without synthetic nitrogen, the world could not feed its current population — but the energy price is enormous.

docs.title: The general shape of the development over time:
docs.text:
Traditional/subsistence farming: EROI is positive — you get more out than you put in Roughly 5–10 calories out per calorie in (human/animal labor, simple tools)

Modern industrial farming: EROI is negative — you put in more than you get out Roughly 10 calories in per 1 calorie of food out when you count everything: diesel for machinery, natural gas for fertilizer production (Haber-Bosch is hugely energy intensive), pesticides, irrigation, transport, processing, refrigeration

  • Pre-1900: EROI ~ 5–10:1 (positive)
  • 1950s: roughly breaking even
  • 1970s onwards: clearly negative, ~ 3–10:1 input/output depending on crop and region
  • Today: up to 10–15:1 for heavily processed food

docs.title: What This Means for the Game
docs.text: In SimFuture, the Minister for Food and Agriculture faces exactly this dilemma. Investments in food security are not free — they carry an energy cost, and that energy cost has consequences for climate and inequality. A region that can shift towards less energy-intensive farming — through soil health, reduced meat consumption, or precision agriculture — can reduce both its emissions and its vulnerability to fossil fuel price shocks.

docs.title: Further Reading
docs.text: F. Krausmann and colleagues at the Institute of Social Ecology, BOKU Vienna have published detailed time series on agricultural energy use, including historical data for Austria going back to 1830. Vaclav Smil, Energy and Civilization: A History (MIT Press, 2017) — a readable and comprehensive account, and Feeding the World David Pimentel (Cornell University), Food, Energy and Society — classic studies on the energy balance of US corn production.

docs.title: Source
docs.text: Background text on agricultural EROI drafted with AI assistance (Claude, Anthropic, 2026). Key sources: Krausmann et al., Institute of Social Ecology BOKU Vienna; V. Smil, Energy and Civilization (MIT Press, 2017); D. Pimentel, Cornell University.

docs.title: Big issues of our time
docs.text: The SDG game creates a space where up to 60 players can discover and explore some of the big issues of our time, and their connections - inequality, poverty, climate change, equal rights for all, food and agriculture, and energy - in a playful way. The game invites you to create a different future to the one we are currently moving towards. We could indeed create a future with less poverty, less inequality, more social freedom within the limits set by our planet, and more prosperity and happiness for all. For us, our children and our grandchildren.

docs.title: Playing
docs.text:
The game is also meant to let layers experience self-determined agency — without lecturing to them. Unfortunately, our lives have increasingly degenerated into mere execution of someone else's plan, with no more room for agency of the self — see Hartmut Rosa’s book Situation und Konstellation.

To dig deeper have a look at Tom DeMarco's Slack and Stefan Kühl's Brauchbare Illegalität.

In this sense, SimFuture tries to be an endless game.

docs.title: Help
docs.text:
It's not easy, but firstly there are clever and committed players in the game and secondly the game supports you at every step with hints, tips, suggestions and questions. And a, hopefully growing, glossary is part of this manual.

The game is divided into 10 geographical regions, each with 6 roles, see People for details.

docs.title: Discussions and negotiations
docs.text:
The game calculates the conditions in each region from now until the end of the century - which is less then 75 years away! The players intervene three times in the course of events to create a different (game) reality for the future: better, more dignified and happier. With each intervention, the players decide which measures they want to invest in and how much they want to invest. Against the backdrop of a limited budget which changes dynamically over time. These discussions and negotiations are the core of the game. They force players to argue face-to-face in favour of their decisions, weigh up the pros and cons of a particular investment plan and make compromises in order to move forward.

Good luck and enjoy.

docs.title: PS: I need your help
docs.text: The manual is originally written in English, German was done by deepl.com and checked by a native speaker. If you are a native speaker of a language other than English or German, and would like to to help bring this manual to your native language, then send a mail to simfuture@blue-way.net. We'd translate the text with Deepl.com, send you the tranlations for review (we're working on you being able to do that with a web-app) and you'd return the reviewed text back to us. There are also lots of links to external sites for deeper understanding. It would be teriffic if you could also find and insert links to sites in your language that cover the information that the English sites do. I know this is a lot of work, but it's for a good cause and your work will be duly noted!

docs.title: This manual can grow
docs.text: This manual is only a beginning. With your help and ideas it can grow into a much more lively document with pictures, videos, lesson plans, etc. Send me your material and ideas, if you want to.

docs.title: Terra3
docs.text: In Germany there is a schoolbook, Terra3, that covers many of the same topics of this game. It may be a good companion.

docs.title: Two scenarios
docs.text:
The game is based on the regional model of the earth4all. project. The project is about two possible futures:

Too Little, Too Late: This scenario reflects our current path. What if societies continue at a similar pace, setting incremental targets but failing to take strong collective action? Will we cross irreversible tipping points and threaten the foundations of the Earth's stable climate? Will increasing inequality lead to a worsening of social tensions?

Giant Leap: This scenario assumes that societies will embark on a new path towards a sustainable world by 2050. What if we fundamentally reshape our economic, energy and food systems so that they work for both people and the planet? Can we avoid the worst impacts of climate change and increase the resilience of our societies to shocks? Will we be able to end extreme poverty and guarantee healthy diets and access to quality education and healthcare for all?

Both descriptions are from the earth4all website, accessed on 12 September 2025

docs.title: Ways to play the game
docs.text:
With this game you can explore some of the big issues of our time: poverty, inequality, equal rights, food and agriculture, and energy, in a playful manner.

Here are some suggestions for how you may emphasize different gameflows through the game for the players. You can, for example, use it to

  • let players learn about the UN Sustainable Development Goals,
  • engage players in conversation, discussion and negotiation,
  • let players experience differences in power,
  • lead players from one simple causal feedbackloop to a systems understanding,
  • or, perhaps best of all, do nothing: start the game and then stand back. Let your players play and watch what they do with the freedom that was given to them.

If you have any other ides, drop us a line.

docs.title: What you need to play
docs.text:
You need a game master or mistress, up to 60 players, a room for everyone and a modern browser on a modern device for everyone: mobile phones, tablets, laptops or PCs.

And of course time. As the discussions are face-to-face, you should plan at least one hour each for the introduction, each round and the debrief. This time can be spread over several days, especially if you play this game with school students.

Finally, you need the start-code: oscar

docs.title: Worker reaction
docs.text:
In any economy, the national income is shared between owners and workers. The sharing is done through negotiation between the two. Take a look at the share going to workers (= labor) in the US. Since 1948, the share has been going down and down and down. The source of this image is here. Currently it is here: The situation is similar in other countries. What do the graphs tell you?

You can give the workers (= the ones who barely live from paycheck to paycheck) a lift: Another way to give workers a lift, is to increase the workers reaction to any demands the owners make. This gives workers more bargaining power vis-a-vis the owners - they become tougher. The policy you set is a percentage increase in the bargaining power. 0 leaves things as they are, you change nothing. 1 increases the power by 1%, 2 by 2% and 3 by 3%. This may not sound like much but it a start to address inequality.

See also Strengthen Unions
